content:
{
  "title": "Counterfactually Augmented Prompting to Reduce Social Biases in LLM Outputs",
  "problem": "Large language models (LLMs) often perpetuate or amplify social biases and stereotypes when generating text, even under seemingly neutral prompts. Existing debiasing methods rely heavily on fine-tuning or post-processing, with prompting alone showing limited effectiveness.",
  "motivation": "As LLMs are increasingly deployed in sensitive applications, reducing social bias at inference time—without costly retraining—has become critical. Prompting is a scalable mitigation strategy, but new methods are needed to improve its effectiveness. This work explores ways to harness LLMs' own counterfactual reasoning abilities to internally correct bias.",
  "method": "We propose Counterfactually Augmented Prompting (CAP), a technique where each prompt is paired with dynamically generated 'counterfactual sub-prompts' targeting potential social biases (e.g., by flipping demographic attributes or explicitly requesting unbiased reasoning). The LLM first answers the original prompt, then answers the counterfactuals, and finally produces a self-reflective synthesis prompt asking the model to identify and remove any bias or stereotype present in its own prior responses. This process leverages model-internal critique and comparative reasoning to guide unbiased output generation, without additional training.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate CAP on Social Bias Benchmarks (e.g., StereoSet, CrowS-Pairs) and open-ended prompt sets. Baselines include standard prompting, instruction prompting, and chain-of-thought debiasing. Metrics include bias scores (e.g., PPL-based, human annotation), utility preservation, and response diversity. Statistical significance will be assessed across multiple model families (GPT-3.5, Llama-2, etc.)."
}
